title: Malvern, Dunar & Wales (Festiniog, Machynlleth)

main_route.excerpt: We set off to Malvern to hear Thomas & Peter playing in the orchestra for the summer concert.
We all left. It took 1½ hours to pack + load food at 8&, by 3.45. we reache Dunar. We had a campfire + there were midges.
We packed up & reached Machynlleth before lunch.

main_route.inference_reason: All trips start and end at home; diary ends at Machynlleth with no further entries in this spread.

excursions.label: Festiniog Railway: Penrhyn to Dduallt and walk back

stops.normalized_name: Penrhyndeudraeth station, Ffestiniog Railway, Wales, UK
stops.excerpt: We take a picnic & get on Festiniog Railway at Penrhyn.

stops.normalized_name: Dduallt station, Ffestiniog Railway, Wales, UK
stops.excerpt: We get off at Dduallt. It's a very, very pretty walk 1½ hours, back to Penrhyn.

unresolved.issue: Uncertain place name; appears to be where the family drove back to for tea after the Festiniog Railway walk; possibly a local hamlet or farm name near Penrhyndeudraeth/Porthmadog area. Could be a misread of the handwriting.
unresolved.excerpt: We drove back to [?Ainas] for tea.

boundary_flags: mixed_start: A_0230_R contains earlier diary entries (bluebell walk at Midsummer Hill) before the May 22nd trip start
mixed_end: A_0232_L contains entries for a subsequent period; the last entry is Wednesday May 27th at Machynlleth
summary: A late-May 1998 Whitsun trip from Birmingham to Malvern for Thomas and Peter's school concert, then on to a campsite called Dunar in Wales for several nights. On Tuesday May 26th the family took a day excursion on the Festiniog Railway from Penrhyn to Dduallt and walked back, before breaking camp on Wednesday and driving to Machynlleth.
extraction_notes: The campsite 'Dunar' is unidentified on modern maps; the diary spelling is clear but the location is approximate (mid/north Wales, consistent with the Ffestiniog Railway area). 'Penrhyn' on the Ffestiniog Railway is most likely Penrhyndeudraeth (the southernmost mainline station). '[?Ainas]' is a uncertain transcription and may be a local farm/hamlet name. The trip has mixed page boundaries at both start and end (A_0230_R, A_0232_L). William's injured foot (bruised, not broken) is a narrative thread. 'Tonie/Tonic' in the diary text is the dog Tinder (phonetic nickname).
